title: The interplay of cloud cover and 3D urban structures reduces human access to sunlight
methods: three-filter sunlight model (day length x cloud x sky-view-factor)
sky view factor from lidar DSM
data_sources: US DOE Building America climate zones

data_scope.spatial_coverage: 1,353 cities in the contiguous United States
data_scope.city_definition: US Census TIGER boundaries refined with ESA WorldCover built-up
data_scope.cloud_trend_span: multi-year GOES/MODIS/ISCCP records (exact span not restated in abstract)

core_conclusion: Urban sunlight is reduced by two stacked filters, cloud cover and 3D building geometry. Across 1,353 US cities for 2020, the average annual loss is 2,896 h (about 121 days): 2,448 h (about 102 days) from cloud cover and 448 h (about 19 days) from 3D urban structures. With cloud cover and vertical urbanization both rising, the deficit is projected to worsen.
why_it_matters: Makes daylight a measurable, budgetable urban resource; disentangles atmospheric vs geometric drivers so cities know which part they can design away; links physical urban form to health-relevant sunlight exposure; shows a worsening trend; provides a reusable satellite-driven pipeline portable to any city.
why_nature: Integrative cross-scale framework coupling astronomy, atmospheric science and urban morphology; space-to-health mechanism with societal weight; continental-scale and transferable rather than a single case; policy-translatable and forward-looking ('right to light').
added_value_to_my_research: Population-weighted sunlight deficit surface -> 2SFCA-style daylight exposure equity metric
Learned 3D-morphology operator with XAI attribution -> explainable SVF surrogate for global transfer
Morphology-cloud feedback as physics-informed graph constraint in spatiotemporal GNN
Daylight vs heat vs PV vs density multi-objective form optimization extending EV efficiency-equity template
Join sunlight-deficit surface to spatial health records via MGWR for a new built-environment health determinant
